title: Nora E. (Gerry) Danforth (1853-1944): A Long Life in the Troy, Maine Farm Country
narrative: Nora E. Gerry was born in October 1853 in Maine, the daughter of Chester B. Gerry and Martha Gerry. The family record places her in the orbit of Troy, in Waldo County, the interior farming town with which her married life would be bound up, though the free records consulted here do not pin down her exact birthplace; 'Maine' is as far as the documentation safely reaches, and the specific town should be treated as probable rather than proven. She was born into a country still close to its frontier beginnings: Troy itself had been settled around 1801 as Bridge's Plantation and incorporated in 1812 as Kingville, passing through the names Joy (1815) and Montgomery (1826) before finally becoming Troy in 1827, the same period in which Waldo County was organized, on February 7, 1827, out of a portion of Hancock County and named for Brigadier-General Samuel Waldo of the Waldo Patent.

The Gerry name is an old one in New England, and in the local geography of Troy the family is echoed in place-names; the town's scattered rural neighborhoods included 'Gerrish Corner' alongside Cooks Corner, East Troy, Greens Corner, Hales Corner, Mitchell Corner, North Troy, Pine Hill, Rogers Corner, Smarts Corner, Troy Center, Ward Hill, and West Troy. Nora's parents, Chester B. and Martha Gerry, are named in the family record, but beyond their names the documentary detail available from free sources is thin: their own birth and death dates, Martha's maiden name, and the full roster of Nora's siblings are not established here and are left open rather than guessed at. What is clear is that Nora grew up in the rural, intermarried world of mid-nineteenth-century Waldo County, a community of a few hundred to roughly fifteen hundred people whose founding families wove together across a handful of surnames.

She married Otis J. Danforth (1840-1895), a man about thirteen years her senior. Their marriage joined her to one of the Danforth households of the Troy area and made her, in the local idiom, 'Mrs. N. E. Danforth.' The precise date and place of the marriage are not confirmed in the free records consulted here - Maine kept marriage records at the town level, and statewide registration did not begin until 1892 - so while the union itself is well attested by the couple's shared life and children, its exact entry in the Troy or Waldo County town books is noted here as a record still to be confirmed rather than quoted. As a married woman Nora would have lived the life typical of a Troy farm wife: the mixed subsistence farming of interior Maine - hay, oats, potatoes, a few cattle and sheep, a kitchen garden, and home production of much that could not be bought - in a town that had no industry of consequence beyond what soil, woodlot, and local mills could yield.

Nora and Otis were the parents of Otis B. Danforth (1871-1941) of Troy, through whom this branch of the family descends. Otis B. Danforth was in turn the father of Marguerite (Danforth) Lenentine, making Nora the grandmother of Marguerite and a direct forebear in the user's line. Whether Nora and Otis J. Danforth had other children is not detailed in the free records gathered here; only the son who carries the line forward, Otis B., is documented in this research, and the rest of the household is left open rather than invented. The recurrence of the father's given name, Otis, across the generations - Otis J. (1840-1895) and his son Otis B. (1871-1941) - is itself a small marker of the family's continuity in Troy.

Otis J. Danforth died in 1895, when Nora was about forty-one or forty-two years old, leaving her a widow for what would prove to be nearly half a century. Widowhood was a common and often long-lasting condition for the women of these rural Maine towns, and Nora's was unusually extended. The decades after 1895 were also a period of steady decline for Troy and towns like it: where Troy had counted roughly 1,484 residents at its mid-century peak around 1850, it had fallen to 766 by 1900 and 638 by 1920, the familiar pattern of rural depopulation as younger people left for the cities, the mills, and the West. Nora remained, by every indication, part of the Troy community through these years. A 'Mrs. N. E. Danforth of Troy' appears as a name in a 1921 Troy newspaper correspondent column already preserved in this family's collection - a notice consistent with Nora in the right place at the right time, and a plausible glimpse of her in her late sixties, though the identification rests on name and place rather than on an explicit statement that this is the same woman.

Nora E. (Gerry) Danforth died in 1944, having lived ninety or ninety-one years. Her exact place of death and place of burial are not confirmed in the free records consulted here; Troy's small cemeteries - among them Hathaway, Mount Holly, Rogers, Webb, and Webster - are the natural places to look for her and her husband's graves, but no specific memorial for her was retrievable from the free, openly accessible sources used in this research, and so her resting place is left as a matter for confirmation rather than asserted. What the record does support is the arc of the life itself.

That arc is remarkable in its span. Born in October 1853, while Franklin Pierce was president and Troy was barely a generation past its frontier naming, Nora lived through the Civil War and its long aftermath as a child and young woman, married into a Waldo County farm family, raised at least the son who would carry the line toward Marguerite Lenentine, was widowed in 1895, and lived on through the slow emptying of the Maine farm towns, the First World War, the 1920s in which her name surfaces in the Troy column, the Great Depression, and into the Second World War before her death in 1944. The firmest facts - her October 1853 birth in Maine to Chester B. and Martha Gerry, her marriage to Otis J. Danforth (1840-1895), her motherhood of Otis B. Danforth (1871-1941), and her death in 1944 - are presented here as documented; the texture around them, drawn from the history of Troy and Waldo County, is offered honestly as context rather than as invented biography, and the genuine gaps in her exact birthplace, marriage record, and burial are named as gaps.
